summary: Fowler critiques the Model Driven Architecture (MDA) claim of 'platform independence' as fundamentally misleading. He points out that Java already provides platform independence from hardware and operating systems, so MDA's claim to be platform-independent while generating Java code is contradictory. MDA merely redefines 'platform' to mean the programming environment, but OMG standards (UML, MOF, XMI) are themselves just another platform. The argument is further weakened by the fact that MDA's Platform Independent Model lacks basic capabilities like I/O libraries, making it less useful than the platforms it claims to abstract away.
key_points: MDA's definition of 'platform' (programming environment) differs from the conventional meaning (hardware/OS), creating a misleading claim
Java already provides hardware and OS platform independence, making MDA's 'platform independence' redundant
OMG standards that comprise MDA's PIM are themselves a platform, so no additional independence is gained
The PIM platform is actually less capable than Java or .NET since it lacks basic functionality like I/O libraries
MDA may have other benefits, but platform independence is not a valid selling point
money_quotes: Hang on a moment, isn't the whole point of Java to be platform independent? So why would I want some platform independent technology that generates another platform independent technology?
When MDA talks about platform independence, it's treating your programming environment as the platform. But this is complete hogwash.
All you are doing is swapping one (hardware/OS) platform independent programming environment for another. You aren't getting any more independence.
How exactly do you do that in the standard OMG PIM platform? Well you can't, because there are no I/O libraries defined in the OMG PIM standards.
The platform independent argument has no foundation.
key_insight: MDA's claim of platform independence is semantically hollow because it merely substitutes one platform abstraction for another while providing less capability than the technologies it generates.
